ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Bhatura; closest match is 'fried bread' (FOODON_03315091). Bhatura is a leavened, deep-fried wheat bread from North India.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bread and similar products — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for leavened deep-fried wheat bread (Bhatura); no exact FoodEx2 code exists for this preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Bhatura. A000E (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Bhatura or equivalent leavened deep-fried Indian bread.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Bhatura is one piece weighing approximately 100g, commonly served alongside Chole (spiced chickpea curry) as part of the Chole Bhature meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutritional values for Bhatura are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods', which provides composition data for Indian wheat-based fried breads. Values reflect a typical deep-fried maida-based bhatura with yogurt leavening. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for comparable fried wheat products. Water content is not reported by ICMR-NIN for this preparation and is set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Bhatura was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 75 is estimated by analogy with refined wheat flour deep-fried bread products (e.g., puri/poori, white bread fried variants) documented in Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care and the University of Sydney GI Database. The fermentation process and yogurt content provide minimal GI moderation. Glycemic load per 100g serving calculated as (75 × 38.5) / 100 = 28.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (350 mg/100g) and calorie-dense deep-fried preparation contributing to weight gain and elevated blood pressure
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Bhatura consumption due to its high sodium content (350 mg per 100g) and calorie density from deep-frying; if consumed, restrict to occasional small portions and avoid adding pickles or high-sodium accompaniments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI ~75) from refined wheat flour (maida) base causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bhatura is made from refined wheat flour with a high GI (~75) and should be avoided or strictly limited by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; if consumed, pair with high-fibre Chole and restrict to a half-portion to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (22g/100g) including saturated fat (~4.5g) and trans fat from deep-frying in vegetable oil, contributing to atherogenic lipid profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high total fat, saturated fat, and potential trans fat content from deep-frying make Bhatura unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; limit to rare occasions and choose smaller portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory potential from refined wheat, dairy (yogurt), and deep-frying in vegetable oils high in omega-6 fatty acids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor their response to Bhatura, as refined grains, dairy, and omega-6-rich frying oils may exacerbate inflammation; occasional consumption is acceptable but daily intake is not recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bhatura is a thick, leavened deep-fried bread originating from the Punjab region of the Indian subcontinent. It is prepared from refined wheat flour (maida) combined with yogurt, baking powder or yeast, and salt; the dough is fermented for several hours before being rolled into discs and deep-fried in hot oil. During frying, the bread puffs up dramatically into a large, hollow, golden sphere with a crispy exterior and a soft, pillowy interior. The fermentation imparts a mild tang to the flavour. Bhatura is most famously paired with Chole (spiced chickpea curry), forming the iconic North Indian meal 'Chole Bhature', typically consumed at breakfast or lunch. Nutritionally, Bhatura is calorie-dense (approximately 380 kcal per 100g) with a high fat content (~22g/100g) due to deep-frying, a moderate protein content (~7.5g/100g) from wheat and yogurt, and a high carbohydrate content (~38.5g/100g) predominantly from refined flour. Its high glycemic index (~75) makes it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood sugar. The high sodium (350 mg/100g) and fat content also raise concerns for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. It is best consumed occasionally as a treat rather than as a daily staple.

confidence_reason: Nutritional values sourced from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods', the primary approved source for Indian dishes. Values are consistent with the known composition of deep-fried refined wheat flour bread. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for comparable fried wheat products. GI estimated by analogy with peer-reviewed University of Sydney GI Database entries for refined wheat fried breads.
notes: water_g set to -1: moisture content for Bhatura as a deep-fried bread is not directly reported in ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation; significant and variable water loss during deep-frying makes a reliable figure unavailable without direct laboratory measurement, and neither approved source provides this value for Bhatura or a sufficiently close analogue. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: Bhatura contains no vitamin C as it is a refined wheat flour and dairy product with no fruit or vegetable components; this is naturally zero. Saturated fat (4.5g), monounsaturated_fat_g (9.0g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (7.0g), trans_fat_g (0.2g), and cholesterol_mg (5.0mg) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for comparable deep-fried refined wheat bread products, as ICMR-NIN does not report detailed fatty acid profiles for Bhatura. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 75 × carbohydrate 38.5g) / 100 = 28.9. The portions array in the input contained three duplicate entries; deduplicated to a single 100g serving in this output. Input health_suitability condition 'diabetes' remapped to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules.
